lens.included_activities: Independent US lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers repeatedly stocking, quoting, selling, and delivering miscellaneous construction inputs such as flat and plate glass, nonwood fencing and accessories, ceiling and gypsum products, building paper, mastics, culvert pipe, storage bins, and architectural metalwork.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufactured-home and prefabricated-building wholesalers whose revenue is predominantly infrequent unit or project sales, manufacturers' captive branches, pure brokers without transferable operations, retailers, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B resale to contractors, fabricators, installers, builders, dealers, and facilities customers, with product gross margin paying for local availability, technical selection, quoting, trade credit, and delivery.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS definition combines recurring stock-and-flow specialty materials with manufactured homes and prefabricated buildings, whose large-ticket, project-like sales cadence and fulfillment economics would make one screen incoherent. The lens retains repeat construction-input distributors and excludes firms dominated by those unit/project categories.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment for all NAICS 423 durable-goods merchant wholesalers rather than 423390 and does not measure AI task exposure. I map its sales and office occupation shares to the narrowed lens's digital quoting, product lookup, order entry, customer communication, credit, purchasing, and inventory work. The bridge may overstate exposure where glass, fencing, or architectural products require drawings and field judgment, and understate it where repetitive quote and submittal preparation occupies technical sellers.
a.rationale: The durable-goods wholesale workforce includes wholesale/manufacturing sales representatives at 16.08% of employment, alongside billing, bookkeeping, customer-service, order, purchasing, inventory, and general office work. The narrowed specialty-material lens adds document-heavy quoting and product-selection tasks suited to AI, but warehouse handling, fabrication interfaces, driving, and delivery remain physical. The range excludes tasks already automated through ERP and e-commerce.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across representative glass, fencing, gypsum, and specialty-material distributors, with current ERP automation and task-level AI trials separated by role.
caveats: The occupational proxy is broader than 423390 and from May 2022.
The retained product categories themselves have different technical-sales intensity.
The supplied labor ratio mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis.

rho.rationale: Within five years, LMM firms can implement copilots for quote drafting, order intake, submittal search, collections, demand planning, and sales prioritization. Conversion is constrained by inconsistent SKUs, supplier catalogs, drawings, local codes, branch practices, ERP integration, and the cost of checking safety- or specification-sensitive recommendations. Large construction-material distributors demonstrate feasibility, but their data and integration resources exceed the lens.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pre/post deployments at 20 or more LMM specialty construction-material distributors measuring labor hours, quote cycle time, error rates, avoided hires, and override frequency by workflow.
caveats: Public examples are scaled enterprises, not LMM firms.
Product-specification errors can create rework or safety liability.
Implementation difficulty, rather than customer price response, is isolated here.

e.rationale: Many independent firms in the retained product categories provide recurring external-customer fulfillment and are operationally transferable. Eligibility is reduced materially because the six-digit code explicitly includes manufactured homes and prefabricated buildings excluded for coherence, and because manufacturer-controlled branches, project-only sellers, concentrated owner books, and weak stand-alone operations do not fit the lens.
e.change_evidence: A business-level classification of the provided 508 LMM-firm estimate by nine-digit product line, repeat-customer revenue, manufacturer affiliation, recurring order frequency, customer concentration, and transferable branch assets.
caveats: No public firm-count split isolates the narrowed retained categories from manufactured homes and prefab buildings.
The provided LMM firm count is itself an EBITDA-margin-bridged estimate.
Some custom glass and architectural-metal sellers may still have project-like economics.

s5.bridge: Broad wholesale-distribution deal sources record transactions across end markets and sizes rather than a five-year control-transfer cohort for eligible 423390 firms. I treat the reported rebound and building-products buyer activity as evidence of a functioning acquisition market, then discount for large-platform concentration, repeat add-ons, internal succession, and untransferable small firms. The proxy may undercount undisclosed private deals but overstate the probability for the narrowed cohort.
s5.rationale: Distribution deal volume increased to 87 transactions in 2026 Q1 from 74 in 2025 Q4, with private-equity-backed activity concentrated in industrial and building products. Active strategic demand supports transferability, but the code's fragmented product mix and absence of an eligible-firm denominator warrant a restrained roughly one-in-five base over five years.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year private-transaction cohort matched to US 423390 firms, with product segment, EBITDA, control status, internal transfers, closures, and repeated platform acquisitions separately identified.
caveats: Published deal counts span multiple distribution industries and geographies.
No 423390 owner-age or succession dataset was found.
Narrow specialty segments may have very different buyer depth.

q.bridge: The Economic Census reports a 36.2% gross margin for NAICS 423390 merchant wholesalers, not AI-benefit retention. I use that observed resale spread to establish that customer contracts are generally product-price based rather than direct reimbursement of labor cost, then discount for bid competition, account repricing, e-commerce transparency, and imitation. The code-wide margin may overstate retention for commodity glass or gypsum and understate it for technical niche products.
q.rationale: An implemented labor saving initially stays inside a bundled product margin. Technical selection, availability, credit, and reliable delivery can preserve some of it, while transparent material pricing and online purchasing push gains toward customers at renewals or rebids. The heterogeneity supports a wide range.
q.change_evidence: Segmented customer-level price, gross-margin, win-rate, and renewal data from AI-adopting and comparison branches over three years, controlling for material inflation, freight, rebates, and product mix.
caveats: Gross margin is not benefit retention.
The 36.2% figure covers excluded manufactured-home and prefab wholesalers as well as retained products.
Direct supplier sales and online marketplaces may accelerate pass-through.

d5.bridge: Census construction spending and NAHB remodeling data measure broad upstream activity, not constant-price quantities for the narrowed miscellaneous-material basket. I translate current construction softness, an aging housing stock, positive real remodeling forecasts, and diverse residential/nonresidential end markets into a modest growth range. Aggregate spending can misstate segment volumes, and inflation can make nominal growth look like quantity growth.
d5.rationale: May 2026 construction spending was 1.5% below May 2025, evidencing cyclicality. Offsetting that, NAHB reports a median owner-occupied home age of 41 years and forecasts real remodeling gains of 3% in 2026 and 2% in 2027. Fencing, glass, interior products, and infrastructure-related items serve varied repair, remodel, and new-build demand, supporting stable-to-moderate five-year quantity growth.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar shipment forecasts for the retained product categories, weighted by 423390 revenue and split across repair/remodel, new residential, nonresidential, and public-infrastructure end markets.
caveats: The demand proxies are much broader than the retained product basket.
Different 423390 categories can move in opposite directions.
Tariffs and commodity-price swings complicate conversion from spending to quantity.

o.rationale: Most year-five quantity still requires a responsible operator to aggregate fragmented supply, check specifications, carry local inventory, extend credit, and coordinate heavy or fragile delivery. Digital self-service can remove manual order-taking, and suppliers or marketplaces can bypass independent branches, but software cannot itself fulfill glass, fencing, gypsum, or architectural components. Segment heterogeneity makes bypass more plausible for standardized products than custom or fragile ones.
o.change_evidence: Product-segment data on manufacturer-direct and marketplace shipment shares, digital self-service adoption, local-stock requirements, delivery claims, and contractor channel switching through 2031.
caveats: Channel displacement may change who operates the service without eliminating the operator role.
Supplier-direct evidence spans distribution categories.
Custom-fabrication interfaces may increase operator need while standardized prefab channels may reduce it.

narrative.executive_view: The retained lens is a collection of repeat specialty-material distributors, not a single uniform product market. Its digital sales and administrative work is meaningfully AI-addressable, while physical inventory, specification accountability, and delivery remain operator-intensive.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate quote and submittal preparation, product matching, order capture, lead selection, credit work, purchasing, and inventory planning. Realization depends on product-master quality and human checks because an incorrect glass, fencing, gypsum, or architectural specification can be costly.
narrative.value_capture: Product-price billing allows initial internal retention of labor savings. Retention varies by segment: technical, fragile, or locally stocked products support service differentiation, while commodity items face stronger bid comparison, online transparency, and supplier-direct pressure.
narrative.firm_availability: Active building-products consolidation indicates buyer demand, but no public cohort measures qualifying 423390 transfers. Eligibility is materially reduced by the decision to exclude manufactured-home and prefab-building specialists and by ordinary concentration and transferability screens.
narrative.demand_durability: The basket participates in repair, remodeling, new construction, and infrastructure, which diversifies but does not eliminate cyclicality. Aging buildings support replacement demand, and most products still need a physical, credit-bearing fulfillment operator even if ordering becomes digital.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The six-digit code is unusually heterogeneous, public occupation and deal evidence is broad, and no revenue weights exist for the narrowed product categories. Construction cycles, tariffs, supplier bypass, specifications liability, fragmented data, and platform consolidation could each dominate AI labor savings.
narrative.principal_driver: AI-addressable quoting and branch administration embedded in recurring, specification-sensitive material fulfillment.
narrative.principal_weakness: The NAICS code's product heterogeneity makes eligibility, demand, and retention estimates unusually dependent on an unavailable segment mix.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 423390 Other Construction Material Merchant Wholesalers
sources.supports: Defines the code and lists its heterogeneous products, including flat glass, fencing, gypsum products, manufactured homes, and nonwood prefabricated buildings.

sources.title: May 2022 OEWS: Merchant Wholesalers, Durable Goods
sources.supports: Reports the broader durable-goods wholesaler occupation mix, including wholesale/manufacturing sales representatives at 16.08% of employment and detailed office occupations.

sources.title: Where value is won and lost in distribution
sources.supports: Describes AI embedded in distributor pricing, procurement, inventory, and customer workflows; construction-material case examples; supplier-direct pressure; and large distribution transactions.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Wholesale Trade Gross Margin Profile
sources.supports: Reports 3,760 merchant-wholesaler firms and a 36.2% gross margin for NAICS 423390 in 2022.

sources.title: Distribution M&A Activity Rebounds in Q1 as Strategic Buyers Accelerate Dealmaking
sources.supports: Reports 87 wholesale-distribution transactions in 2026 Q1 versus 74 in 2025 Q4 and concentration of private-equity-backed activity in industrial and building products.

sources.title: The next normal in construction material distribution
sources.supports: Describes distributors' inventory, logistics, and credit roles and the pressure from online marketplaces, supplier-direct sales, price transparency, and direct-to-site delivery.

sources.title: Monthly Construction Spending, May 2026
sources.supports: Reports May 2026 total construction spending 1.5% below May 2025 and first-five-month spending 2.7% below the comparable 2025 period.

sources.title: 2026 Housing Outlook: Ongoing Challenges, Cautious Optimism and Incremental Gains
sources.supports: Reports home improvement at 45% of residential construction spending in 2025 Q3 and forecasts real remodeling growth of 3% in 2026 and 2% in 2027.
